sections.header: Verses 1-3 — "Come, Let Us Return to the LORD"

heb.paragraph: The cohortative form of shuv ('to return/repent') is the key verb of Hosea. Israel's penitential song uses the right word — shuv is exactly what YHWH has been demanding — but as vv. 4-6 reveal, the repentance is superficial. The form is correct; the substance is lacking. This creates the chapter's central irony: Israel mouths the vocabulary of repentance without the heart change it requires.

From the root hyh ('to live'). The claim that God will revive 'after two days' and raise up 'on the third day' has generated extensive christological interpretation. In context, however, it likely reflects Israel's presumptuous confidence in quick restoration: they expect God to heal them on a predictable schedule, as if divine forgiveness operated on a timetable.

refs.note: Jesus' resurrection 'on the third day' has been connected to this passage by early church interpreters, though the original context is about Israel's presumptuous repentance.
Isaiah's resurrection promise ('your dead will live, their bodies will rise') uses similar language in a more clearly eschatological context.
The psalmist's confidence that 'weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning' reflects genuine faith rather than formulaic presumption.

notes.note: The call 'Come, let us return' uses the right language but, as the following verses show, reflects shallow remorse rather than deep repentance. Israel has learned the vocabulary of faith without internalizing its meaning.

The 'third day' language has been applied typologically to Christ's resurrection by many interpreters. However, in its original context, it reflects Israel's confident assumption that restoration would come quickly and automatically, without the kind of radical heart change that YHWH demands.

The comparison of God's coming to the certainty of dawn and spring rain is theologically correct but existentially shallow. Israel speaks of 'knowing' God (the very thing they lack, 4:1, 6) as if it could be achieved by liturgical recitation rather than covenantal transformation.

Calvin treated these verses as an example of false repentance: words that sound right but are not accompanied by genuine turning of the heart. The church must always examine whether its pious language reflects true spiritual reality or mere religious convention.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase yehayenu miyyomayim bayyom hashelishi yeqimenu ('he will revive us after two days; on the third day he will raise us up') has been read messianically but in context more likely reflects Israel's expectation of swift, automatic restoration. The temporal specificity is the problem: genuine repentance does not dictate terms to God.

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart identifies this as a penitential liturgy, possibly drawn from an actual worship form used at northern shrines. The form is correct — confession, trust, desire for knowledge of God — but the substance is lacking. Stuart compares it to Saul's confessions in 1 Samuel, which were consistently followed by unchanged behavior.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The penitential song is one of the most debated passages in Hosea. Some scholars read it as genuine (rejected by a too-harsh God), others as shallow (rightly rejected). Andersen and Freedman argue that the key is the word 'acknowledge' (yada) in v. 3: Israel claims to be pursuing the very knowledge it has rejected (4:1, 6). The claim rings hollow against the book's prior evidence.

hist.context: The penitential song (vv. 1-3) directly follows YHWH's withdrawal in 5:15 and appears to be the repentant response God was waiting for. The language is beautiful: return, healing, revival, resurrection, acknowledgment of God, the certainty of his coming like spring rain. Yet the divine response in vv. 4-6 reveals that something is fundamentally wrong with this prayer. It is too glib, too confident, too formulaic. Israel treats repentance as a technique that will automatically produce restoration, without genuine moral transformation. The 'two days / third day' formula may reflect liturgical convention: a prescribed period of penitence followed by guaranteed restoration, reducing the living God to a predictable mechanism.

heb.paragraph: In v. 6, hesed appears alongside daʿat elohim as what YHWH desires instead of sacrifice. This is the most programmatic statement of Hosea's theology and one of the most important verses in the prophetic corpus. Hesed is not sentimentality but covenant faithfulness: the enduring, self-giving loyalty that sustains relationships through betrayal and restoration. Jesus quotes this verse twice (Matt 9:13; 12:7).

refs.note: Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6 when challenged about eating with sinners: 'Go and learn what this means: I desire mercy, not sacrifice.'
Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6 a second time when his disciples are accused of Sabbath violation.
Samuel's parallel declaration: 'To obey is better than sacrifice, and to heed is better than the fat of rams.'
Micah's parallel: 'What does the LORD require? To act justly, love mercy, walk humbly with your God.'

notes.note: The morning mist and early dew metaphors capture the essential problem of Israel's repentance: it is genuine in the moment but lacks endurance. Heat (testing, temptation, cultural pressure) dissipates it completely.

This verse does not reject the sacrificial system but establishes a hierarchy of values: covenant loyalty and knowledge of God are the substance; sacrifice is the form. Form without substance is worse than useless. Jesus' two citations of this verse show its centrality to his own ethic of mercy.

The catalogue of sins is geographically specific: Adam (a town at the Jordan crossing, Josh 3:16), Gilead, and Shechem. Each location represents a site of covenant violation — murder, ambush, and priestly corruption. The emphasis on bloodshed shows that covenant unfaithfulness has moved beyond religious syncretism into social violence.

The 'horrible thing' (shaʿaruriyyah) in Israel is the comprehensive nature of the corruption: it is not isolated incidents but systemic prostitution that has 'defiled' the entire nation. The brief mention of Judah's 'harvest' (v. 11) may be either a threat (judgment is coming) or a promise (restoration is appointed).
Calvin used the mist metaphor to warn against temporary religious enthusiasm: many people experience flashes of devotion that evaporate when tested. Genuine faith endures through heat.

Calvin insisted this verse does not abolish ceremonial law but prioritizes the moral and relational dimensions it was designed to serve. Sacrifice without mercy is not merely inadequate but offensive to God.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The term tal ('dew') refers to the heavy moisture condensation common in Israel that appears overnight but evaporates quickly in the morning sun. As a metaphor for Israel's loyalty, it emphasizes beauty that lacks durability.

The construction hesed hafatsti welo-zevah uses the negative particle lo not as absolute negation but as comparative negation: 'I desire mercy more than sacrifice.' The same construction appears in Prov 8:10: 'Choose instruction rather than silver.'

stuart.source: Douglas Stuart, Hosea-Jonah, WBC (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Stuart argues that v. 6 is the hermeneutical key to the entire prophetic movement. The pre-literary prophets (Samuel, Elijah, Elisha) enacted this principle; Hosea articulates it; and later prophets (Isaiah, Micah, Jeremiah) develop it. The verse establishes that the sacrificial system is instrumental, not ultimate: it serves the covenant relationship and has no value apart from it.

The geographical catalogue of sin sites creates a 'map of corruption' covering central Israel: Adam at the Jordan, Gilead in Transjordan, Shechem in the central highlands. Murder, ambush, and covenant violation characterize every region. The 'defilement' (tame) of v. 10 uses purity language to describe the comprehensive contamination of the land.

andersen_freedman.source: Francis I. Andersen & David Noel Freedman, Anchor Bible — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The divine response to Israel's penitential song is the most theologically concentrated passage in Hosea. The mist/dew simile (v. 4), the hesed/da'at declaration (v. 6), and the geographical catalogue (vv. 7-11) together constitute Hosea's answer to the question: why is Israel's repentance insufficient? The answer is that shuv ('return') requires not liturgical recitation but transformative encounter with the character of God as expressed in hesed and da'at.

hist.context: God's response to the penitential song is devastating. The love of Ephraim and Judah is compared to morning mist and early dew: it appears briefly and evaporates under the first heat. This is the diagnosis of superficial repentance. Verse 6 is Hosea's most famous statement and one of the theological foundations of the prophetic tradition: 'I desire mercy (hesed), not sacrifice, and acknowledgment (daʿat) of God rather than burnt offerings.' This does not abolish the sacrificial system but subordinates it to the relational realities it was meant to express. Without hesed and daʿat, sacrifice is empty ritual. The remainder of the chapter catalogues specific violations: covenant breaking at Adam, murder at Gilead, priestly ambush at Shechem, and the 'horrible thing' in Israel — Ephraim's thoroughgoing prostitution that has defiled the land.

rows.label: vv.1-3
rows.text: Penitential song: Israel's call to return

rows.label: vv.4
rows.text: Divine response: morning mist and dew

rows.label: vv.10-11
rows.text: Defilement in Israel, harvest for Judah

lit.note: The chapter pairs a penitential song (vv. 1-3) with its divine critique (vv. 4-11), creating a dialogue between superficial repentance and God's demand for genuine covenant transformation. Verse 6 is the theological fulcrum, quoted by Jesus as the hermeneutical key to his ministry.

themes.note: Mercy scores high because hesed is the central term of the chapter. Worship is prominent due to the sacrifice-versus-mercy dialectic. Justice remains strong because the geographical catalogue documents specific violations of social order.

ppl.name: Hosea
ppl.text: Verse 6 is Hosea's most famous statement and one of the theological foundations of the prophetic tradition: 'I desire mercy (hesed), not sacrifice, and acknowledgment (daʿat) of God rather than burnt offerings…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Morning Mist: Israel's Shallow Repentance

translations.text: Let us acknowledge the Lord; let us press on to acknowledge him. As surely as the sun rises, he will appear; he will come to us like the winter rains, like the spring rains that water the earth.”
Let us know; let us press on to know the LORD; his going out is sure as the dawn; he will come to us as the showers, as the spring rains that water the earth.”
As marauders lie in ambush for a victim, so do bands of priests; they murder on the road to Shechem, carrying out their wicked schemes.
As robbers lie in wait for a man, so the priests band together; they murder on the way to Shechem; they commit villainy.

thread.text: Jesus' resurrection 'on the third day' has been connected to this passage by early church interpreters, though the original context is about Israel's presumptuous repentance.
Isaiah's resurrection promise ('your dead will live, their bodies will rise') uses similar language in a more clearly eschatological context.
The psalmist's confidence that 'weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning' reflects genuine faith rather than formulaic presumption.
Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6 when challenged about eating with sinners: 'Go and learn what this means: I desire mercy, not sacrifice.'
Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6 a second time when his disciples are accused of Sabbath violation.
Samuel's parallel declaration: 'To obey is better than sacrifice, and to heed is better than the fat of rams.'

coaching.tip: 'Come, let us return to the LORD' (v. 1) — finally, repentance! But is it genuine? The beautiful words ('he will heal us... raise us up... revive us') are followed by God's devastating assessment: 'Your love is like a morning cloud... like the dew that goes early away' (v. 4). They know the right words but lack staying power. Shallow repentance evaporates.

'I desire steadfast love and not sacrifice, the knowledge of God rather than burnt offerings' (v. 6). Jesus quotes this twice (Matt 9:13, 12:7). It's not anti-ritual but anti-hypocrisy: sacrifice without covenant loyalty is empty performance. God wants the heart that produces the ritual, not ritual that masks a wandering heart.
